summary: Cathy O'Neil endorses Campaign Zero's call to end Broken Windows policing, arguing that this approach has criminalized communities of color and created feedback loops that perpetuate bias. She critiques a New York Times study on police shootings for ignoring how uneven policing increases the likelihood of police interactions for Black Americans. Using NYC's Stop and Frisk as a case study, she shows how the policy disproportionately targeted minorities while being ineffective at addressing violent crime. O'Neil connects this to predictive policing algorithms, explaining how biased data from these encounters gets fed into systems that compound the original discrimination in sentencing and parole decisions.
key_points: Broken Windows policing leads to over-policing of minority communities and excessive force in minor situations
Studies on police shooting bias are incomplete if they don't account for the vastly higher chance of police interactions for Black Americans
NYC's Stop and Frisk stopped young Black men more times than there were young Black men in the city, yet only 1 in 1000 stops linked to violent crime
Biased policing data feeds into predictive policing and sentencing algorithms, creating self-reinforcing cycles of discrimination
The Broken Windows theory was never proven effective and optimizes for the wrong definition of success
money_quotes: It's much more likely for blacks, especially young black men, to end up in an interaction with cops. And that's due in large part to the broken theory of Broken Windows policing.
The number of stops of young black men exceeded the entire city population of young black men (168,126 as compared to 158,406).
About 1 in 1000 such stops are directly linked to a violent crime.
When City Hall decides to use predictive policing based on this data, they end up over policing the same neighborhoods, creating even more uneven and biased data.
That continuing stream of data even ends up in sentencing and paroling algorithms, making it more likely for those same over-policed populations to stay in jail longer.
It's high time we get rid of the root cause, the theory of Broken Windows, which was never proven in the first place.
key_insight: Broken Windows policing creates a vicious cycle where biased enforcement generates biased data that then feeds algorithmic systems, compounding discrimination at every stage from patrol routes to prison sentences.
